canonical_examples.analysis: Mill's procedure is recognizably steelmanning even though the modern label is later: he does not treat Christianity as the weakest superstition available to ridicule. He reconstructs it as a serious moral doctrine containing partial truths about self-renunciation, humility, and inward moral discipline, then argues that it is incomplete when it suppresses pagan virtues such as magnanimity, public spirit, and self-assertion. The response gains force because it first states what Christianity contributes at its best before criticizing its one-sidedness.
canonical_examples.problem: In On Liberty, John Stuart Mill argues that even doctrines one believes false must be heard because adversaries may hold part of the truth; his discussion of Christian and pagan ethics reconstructs Christianity's moral strength before criticizing its incompleteness.

canonical_examples.analysis: Rapoport's rules operationalize steelmanning: first restate the target's position so clearly that the target can recognize it; then identify points of agreement; then specify what has been learned; only then introduce disagreement. Applied to a dispute, this turns the opponent from a caricature into the strongest available argument. The critic must answer the best version of the view, including its true motivations and strongest evidence, rather than the easiest formulation.
canonical_examples.problem: Anatol Rapoport's debate rules, later popularized by Daniel Dennett, require a critic to express an opponent's position clearly, list agreements, and acknowledge learning before rebuttal.

core_claims: A criticism is epistemically weak if it defeats only a version of the opposing view that competent advocates would reject.
The strongest reconstruction of a view may be more coherent, qualified, and evidence-sensitive than any single opponent's actual wording.
Understanding an opponent's central motivation and success conditions is a prerequisite for locating the real disagreement.
A good rebuttal should leave the opponent saying, in effect, that their view has been understood before it has been challenged.
Agreement-finding is not concession theater; it isolates the exact proposition, inference, or priority where disagreement remains.
Steelmanning can improve one's own view by importing the rival's strongest insight before deciding whether the rival conclusion follows.
description: Steelmanning is the discipline of reconstructing the strongest defensible version of an opposing view before evaluating or rebutting it, replacing caricature with charitable, adversarially robust understanding.
failure_modes: Inventing a position the opponent does not hold: excessive reconstruction can replace the real dispute with a more sophisticated thesis that lacks social or textual accountability.
False symmetry: strengthening every side can imply that all positions deserve equal standing even when evidence, expertise, or harm is asymmetrical.
Conflict avoidance: the practice can become endless restatement and agreement-finding that never reaches the hard objection.
Status laundering: a polished steelman may make a bad argument seem more credible than its actual warrants allow.
Misplaced charity toward strategic bad faith: adversaries can exploit steelmanning by letting critics supply rigor, qualifications, and evidence they never accepted.

key_vocabulary.Charitable reconstruction: Restating a view in its most plausible coherent form while preserving its actual commitments.
key_vocabulary.Crux: The proposition, value, or inference on which the disagreement most depends; if it changed, the overall view would likely change.
key_vocabulary.Ideological Turing Test: A test of whether one can present an opposing view so well that its adherents cannot easily distinguish the presentation from one by an insider.
key_vocabulary.Principle of Charity: The interpretive norm of reading others as making true, rational, or coherent claims where the evidence permits.
key_vocabulary.Rapoport's Rules: A sequence for criticism: restate the opponent, note agreements, report what was learned, and only then rebut.
key_vocabulary.Steelman: The strongest defensible version of a position, often stronger than its first or most common presentation.
key_vocabulary.Straw man: A weakened or distorted version of a position that is easier to refute than the real view.
key_vocabulary.Sympathetic objection: A criticism that preserves the target view's best motivations and evidence while challenging its decisive move.

name: Steelmanning
operative_procedure: State the opponent's thesis in neutral language, separating the claim itself from tone, tribal markers, and poor presentations.
Identify the view's strongest motivation: the real problem, value, evidence, or harm that makes intelligent people find it compelling.
Replace weak premises with the strongest premises the view can legitimately use while staying faithful to its identity.
Add necessary qualifications, scope limits, and distinctions that a competent defender would make.
Run an Ideological Turing Test: ask whether an informed advocate would recognize the reconstruction as fair and forceful.
List substantive agreements and the parts of the rival view that improve or constrain your own position.
Find the crux: isolate the smallest number of claims, tradeoffs, or inferences on which your disagreement actually turns.
Respond to the steelman directly, making clear which strengthened premise, inference, empirical claim, or value priority you reject.
Revise your own position if the steelman exposes a true omission, then report the remaining disagreement without reverting to the weaker target.
scope_and_limits: Steelmanning is strongest in intellectual disagreement, product and policy debates, peer review, negotiation, and conflict where the goal is truth-tracking or durable mutual understanding. It is less appropriate when rapid protective action is needed, when the target view is already precisely specified, or when bad-faith actors use charitable reconstruction to evade responsibility for what they actually said or did.
source_summary: Authored from model knowledge of Mill, Rapoport, Dennett, and contemporary rationalist debate norms, without fetched source text.

source_texts.title: Anatol Rapoport, Fights, Games, and Debates
Daniel C. Dennett, Intuition Pumps and Other Tools for Thinking

when_to_use: A disagreement is stuck because each side is answering a caricature or the weakest public representative of the other side.
You need to criticize a view while preserving trust with people who hold it.
A decision depends on whether a rival position has a real insight that your current view is missing.
A debate contains heat, slogan substitution, or tribal sorting that obscures the actual crux.
You are preparing a rebuttal and need it to survive review by the strongest informed defenders of the target view.
